text: The patient is positioned in lithotomy, with the perineal region exposed using traction sutures. A wide transverse perineal skin incision is made, followed by dissection of the perineal body to reach the fistula tract. The surgeon carefully avoids perforating the rectum, using a finger inside the anal canal for guidance. The anovaginal fistula tract is sectioned with scissors, revealing the fistula orifice in the anal canal. Blunt dissection of the rectovaginal septum continues, and the vaginal fistula orifice is resected. The edges of the rectal fistula orifice are trimmed, and a primary closure is performed with simple sutures from top to bottom. The anterior rectal wall is applied to reinforce the suture and increase basal pressure tone in the anal canal.

The surgeon minimally dissects the retracted ends of the external anal sphincter, ensuring they can overlap without causing nerve damage. The sphincteroplasty is completed by overlapping one end above the other to address severe anal incontinence.

An elliptical skin incision is made in the right outer lip, followed by mobilization of a flap including the bulbocavernous muscle and surrounding adipose tissue. The flap is carefully detached posteriorly to preserve the posterior inferior vascular pedicle, which supplies blood from the internal pudendal artery. The liberated flap is interposed between the posterior vaginal wall and the anal canal, with the skin of the flap intact to reconstruct the posterior vaginal wall and perineum. The skin edge of the flap is sutured to the posterior wall of the vagina.
The surgeon sutures the flap to close the perineal defect and closes the wound in the outer lip, completing the repair of the anovaginal fistula and addressing anal incontinence.
